Maybe we pay them a percentage — say, half their regular salary — while offering them good training and assistance for eight months to find another job. At the same time, we'd convince management to shift to a less profit-driven focus.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Exit is one option among several — remediation comes first.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The proposed program is active: distressed-asset team involvement, management engagement on mindset, and workforce transition planning.

options.A: The Atsui lobbying strategy leverages financial threats against the government.
options.B: the framing that if the government's rigid labor laws prevent cutting 50% of the workforce, all 100% of the jobs may be at risk because the company won't survive if tariffs are reduced to 20%.
options.C: No lobbying is possible in Atsui given the rigid labor law framework.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The approach is reasoned framing, not threats.
rationales.B:
Full statement: the framing that if the government's rigid labor laws prevent cutting 50% of the workforce, all 100% of the jobs may be at risk because the company won't survive if tariffs are reduced to 20% — government doesn't want factory shutdown, so there may be room for negotiated conclusion.

Correct. Per Head of Equities: 'If we do try to salvage the situation and continue with our investment, there's a path that involves the government. Our pitch should be that if there are legal roadblocks for cutting 50% of the jobs, you might be putting 100% of the jobs at risk because the company won't survive if tariffs are reduced to 20%. The government doesn't want the factory to shut down because of its rigid labor laws, so there may well be room for a more, let's say, negotiated conclusion.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Lobbying is an option — framed as: rigid rules blocking a 50% workforce cut may put 100% of the jobs at risk if the company fails.

options.A: R-SWF should ignore AOG's rumored contract renegotiation as immaterial.
options.B: R-SWF should escalate the AOG renegotiation rumor directly to arbitration.
options.C: handle with a positive collaborative approach — 'we don't want a disgruntled operator. Happy employees, happy travelers, better experience, more traffic'.

rationales.A: Incorrect. An operator's dissatisfaction is a genuine operational risk.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The preferred posture is collaborative — a disgruntled operator harms the asset; happy employees and travelers make a better airport.
rationales.C:
Full statement: handle with a positive collaborative approach — 'we don't want a disgruntled operator. Happy employees, happy travelers, better experience, more traffic'; if AOG activates break clause, R-SWF is responsible for finding new operator; wait for delay talks to resolve then collaborate.

Correct. Per the Head of Infrastructure: 'We all signed a well-structured agreement, and that affords us some decent protection against any meddling in the fee structure, though there are break clauses if anything gets too out of line. Still, there are incentives built into the concession agreement to make sure everyone wins to a greater or lesser extent when traffic goes up. Equally, we don't want a disgruntled operator. Happy employees, happy travelers, better experience, more traffic... AOG is a strong global operator. If they did activate a break clause in two or three years' time, that lands us with a responsibility we really don't want, which is finding a new operator. We're still satisfied with their cooperation. I recommend seeing how talks over the delays play out.'

options.A: Construction cost overrun risk for the airport carries no mitigation in the deal structure.
options.B: fixed-price construction contract awarded under tender — passing penalties for delayed or subpar construction to the contractor.
options.C: Overrun risk is managed through cost-plus contracting that reimburses the builder's actual expenses.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The structure addresses it contractually.
rationales.B:
Full statement: fixed-price construction contract awarded under tender — passing penalties for delayed or subpar construction to the contractor; ±10% CapEx sensitivity applied in modeling.

Correct. Per the reading: 'There are some sensitivities around CapEx, and we're looking to manage this risk through a fixed-price contract, the language of which says that whatever penalties we'd face for delayed or subpar construction will be passed down to the contractor. We've applied a ±10% sensitivity around that.' Fixed-price contract structure passes cost overrun risk to contractor, protecting R-SWF's IRR from CapEx uncertainty.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Cost-plus would EXPOSE the project to overruns; the tendered FIXED-PRICE contract passes delay and quality penalties to the contractor.

options.A: R-SWF has no protection regarding concession fees or quality standards at the airport.
options.B: The concession agreement contains no contractual protections of any kind.
options.C: concession agreement includes quality and performance standards to be met for design/construction/development (including timely delivery of new terminal) and operations.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Protections exist and are contractual.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The concession agreement embeds quality and performance standards across design, construction, and development — including timely delivery.
rationales.C:
Full statement: concession agreement includes quality and performance standards to be met for design/construction/development (including timely delivery of new terminal) and operations; break clauses for out-of-line performance.

Correct. Per Memo 1B and Head of Infrastructure: 'Concession agreement includes quality and performance standards to be met for design/construction/development (including timely delivery of new terminal) and operations, respectively... We all signed a well-structured agreement, and that affords us some decent protection against any meddling in the fee structure, though there are break clauses if anything gets too out of line.' Contractual quality/performance standards drive risk sharing.

options.A: With only a 35% minority stake in ABC, R-SWF holds no governance controls and must accept whatever decisions the founder makes without board representation.
options.B: two board seats (from 5 to 7 board members), structured incentives so founder aligns with growth and good oversight (founder retains 65% skin in the game), ongoing due diligence, and being the ones implementing modernization for enhanced monitoring.
options.C: R-SWF exercises no oversight of ABC's management whatsoever, having negotiated purely economic rights with no board participation or incentive alignment.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Minority position notwithstanding, controls were negotiated.
rationales.B: Correct. Per the reading: 'I see your point that we'd only hold 35% of ABC, but we'll also hold two board seats. I can't predict the future, but we've done a lot of due diligence and we've done a lot of interviews with management, customers, and suppliers. We'll structure it so that they are incentivized in alignment with growth and good oversight. After all, they'll still hold 65%. We think they'll see that working with us will create success and that willful mismanagement or corruption or taking too much money out of the business works against them in the long run.' Head of Equities: 'With management having this much skin in the game, their interests are aligned with ours.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Two of seven board seats plus founder incentive structures aligned to growth and oversight constitute substantive governance.

options.A: The ABC exit succeeded purely through financial engineering of the sale structure.
options.B: No exit from ABC proved possible.
options.C: the strategic buyer had a natural fit — an international beverage company exporting to Atsui whose sales were affected by weaker Atsui currency saw producing locally as advantageous (natural FX hedge).

rationales.A: Incorrect. Structure helped less than fit.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The secondary sale closed — the strategic buyer, an international beverage company already exporting to Atsui and hurt by the weak local currency, had natural synergies.
rationales.C:
Full statement: the strategic buyer had a natural fit — an international beverage company exporting to Atsui whose sales were affected by weaker Atsui currency saw producing locally as advantageous (natural FX hedge); R-SWF avoided diplomatic crisis and reputational damage at $27M.

Correct. Per the Year 5 update: 'The PE team was able to avoid a diplomatic crisis and reputational risk damage by finding a buyer for the 35% stake. They sold the full position at $27 million. The stake was sold to an international beverage company that had been exporting to Atsui. The company's sales had been adversely affected by a weaker Atsui currency. Thus, producing locally is advantageous because it provides a natural foreign exchange hedge.' The strategic buyer's own currency hedging motive created a compelling fit.

options.A: The recommended response to the health-foods trend is immediate exit from the ABC position.
options.B: The recommended response is doubling down exclusively on carbonated beverages.
options.C: develop new healthy alternatives (leverage experience producing natural mango drink, expand to sports drinks and juices).

rationales.A: Incorrect. Exit is premature; product adaptation comes first.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Concentration in the declining category is the risk itself — the path is healthy alternatives, leveraging the natural mango drink experience into sports drinks and juices.
rationales.C:
Full statement: develop new healthy alternatives (leverage experience producing natural mango drink, expand to sports drinks and juices) — differentiate to increase market share as consumer preferences shift.

Correct. Per the guideline answer: 'R-SWF can manage the risk of carbonated beverages falling out of favor due to an increasing preference for health foods by working to develop new healthy alternatives to carbonated, presumably sugar-filled drinks. As the production facility expands its ability to produce product, ABC could focus its new product development on healthy alternatives. The company can leverage its experience producing such beverages given the success of its natural mango drink in order to differentiate itself and increase market share.'

options.A: end the practice through discussions with management, remove incentives to cut corners, and inform their thinking about the long-term costs of shortcuts.
options.B: The recommended action on ABC's river dumping is to ignore the issue as immaterial to returns.
options.C: The recommended action is to increase production volume, accepting greater pollution as a cost of growth.

rationales.A:
Full statement: end the practice through discussions with management, remove incentives to cut corners, and inform their thinking about the long-term costs of shortcuts — 'in the long run, regulations catch up to you and the cost of pre-treatment or appropriate handling of waste is much lower than the penalties.'

Correct. Per the reading: 'Head of PE: Ending that practice is an important piece of our talks with management, and so is removing incentives to cut corners... Head of Equities: Our experience in other developing nations as well as developed nations tells us that you'll save some costs in the short term with actions like dumping waste directly into water bodies, but in the long run, regulations catch up to you and the cost of pre-treatment or appropriate handling of waste is much lower than the penalties you get for taking such shortcuts.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The practice must end — reputational and community stakes make it material.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The direction is remediation: end the dumping, remove corner-cutting incentives, and reframe management's long-term thinking.

options.A: The impact-investing framing for ABC is a device for reducing the fund's reporting obligations.
options.B: reframing that recognizes education value in developing markets (proper waste disposal, valuing rivers for fishing/enjoyment) — 'in some less developed areas, they think it's maybe not a big deal to throw things into the river.
options.C: The impact framing means abandoning financial goals in favor of purely social objectives.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The framing broadens the investment thesis rather than shrinking disclosure.
rationales.B:
Full statement: reframing that recognizes education value in developing markets (proper waste disposal, valuing rivers for fishing/enjoyment) — 'in some less developed areas, they think it's maybe not a big deal to throw things into the river; can we inform their thinking?' The CIO explores whether investing has broader impact utility.

Correct. Per CIO: 'We talked about this being a learning experience. We also talked about it displaying aspects of impact investment. Maybe part of the value is in education. In some less developed areas, they think it's maybe not a big deal to throw things into the river. Can we inform their thinking with the idea that wanting a beautiful river for fishing and enjoyment is a virtue and that it's not really that hard to dispose of waste properly? What can we intelligently say about impact?'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Impact COMPLEMENTS financial goals — education value around waste disposal and river stewardship in a developing market alongside returns.

options.A: The Head of PE proposes immediate mass firing of ABC's factory workforce with no transition support.
options.B: The Head of PE proposes no support of any kind for transitioning workers.
options.C: half salary + 8 months of training/job search assistance — trying to convince management to shift from purely profit-driven focus to a workforce-sensitive approach.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Abrupt mass termination is the reputational scenario the fund is trying to avoid.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The proposal is substantial: half salary plus eight months of training and job-search assistance, shifting management from a purely profit-driven posture.
rationales.C:
Full statement: half salary + 8 months of training/job search assistance — trying to convince management to shift from purely profit-driven focus to a workforce-sensitive approach; 'we're going to be part of a sensible transition.'

Correct. Per the reading: 'Maybe we pay them a percentage — say, half their regular salary — while offering them good training and assistance for eight months to find another job. At the same time, we'd convince management to shift to a less profit-driven focus.' Also: 'We know that cutting employees is sensitive. But by helping many more people than we let go and by giving employees proper training so they have the skills for whatever they're doing next, we're going to be part of a sensible transition.'

options.A: dedicating an entire internal investment committee meeting to identifying and discussing potential risks of two relatively small investment opportunities.
options.B: R-SWF's principal ERM strength was sophisticated quantitative risk modeling of the direct investments.
options.C: R-SWF's ERM strength was fully delegated decision-making that kept senior management out of small deals.

rationales.A:
Full statement: dedicating an entire internal investment committee meeting to identifying and discussing potential risks of two relatively small investment opportunities — ample time was taken to allow senior management to express concerns and discuss mitigation.

Correct. Per the reading's guideline answer: 'One of the main strengths of R-SWF's risk management process is that R-SWF dedicated an entire internal investment committee meeting to identifying and discussing the potential risks of two relatively small investment opportunities. Ample time was taken to allow senior management of R-SWF to express their concerns and discuss mitigation strategies to reduce potential risks. The investment committee was able to identify various potential risk factors, and senior management voted on both investment opportunities.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Quantification was actually an identified WEAKNESS; the strength was process attention.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The strength ran the other way — senior attention: a full internal investment committee meeting devoted to risks of two relatively small investments.

options.A: Reputational and ESG risks should be left for review after investments are already made.
options.B: identify potential reputational risks and ESG considerations prior to making investments, particularly for smaller direct investments where financial risk is limited but reputational impact could be outsized (as demonstrated by ABC's fired workers/soap/waste dumping issues).
options.C: Reputational and ESG risk identification applies only to public equities, not direct investments.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The recommendation is identification PRIOR to investing.
rationales.B: Correct. Per the reading: 'Since both investments were quite small in the overall scheme and had limited financial and liquidity risk implications for the fund, more consideration could have been given to identifying potential reputational risks and ESG.' The ABC case demonstrated the outsized reputational risk from small investments.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The guidance applies with particular force to smaller DIRECT investments, where such risks can outweigh financial ones.

options.A: The overarching ERM conclusion is that R-SWF's framework requires no changes, since both direct investments ultimately resolved without loss to the fund.
options.B: The overarching recommendation is eliminating the investment committee process entirely, replacing deliberative review with automated quantitative screening of all direct investments.
options.C: combine strengths (dedicated committee time, senior management input, systematic voting) with improvements: quantitative risk quantification, formal scenario analysis (base/optimistic/pessimistic), pre-investment action plans conditional on bad outcomes, and systematic identification of reputational and ESG risks.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Improvements are recommended alongside retained strengths.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The committee process is a strength to keep — augmented by better quantitative risk measurement and earlier reputational/ESG screening.
rationales.C:
Full statement: combine strengths (dedicated committee time, senior management input, systematic voting) with improvements: quantitative risk quantification, formal scenario analysis (base/optimistic/pessimistic), pre-investment action plans conditional on bad outcomes, and systematic identification of reputational and ESG risks — enabling more proactive, less reactive risk management.

Correct. The reading recommends R-SWF preserve its strength (comprehensive committee-level engagement, senior expert input, systematic voting) while addressing key gaps: (1) quantitative risk quantification alongside qualitative discussion; (2) formal scenario analysis with base/optimistic/pessimistic cases; (3) pre-investment action plans conditional on adverse outcomes; (4) upfront identification of reputational and ESG risks. This mirrors the reading's guideline answer emphasizing that the strengths should be maintained while addressing quantification, scenario analysis, action planning, and ESG/reputational risks.

options.A: The ERM recommendation is to de-emphasize ESG considerations in future direct investments.
options.B: The recommendation is to focus exclusively on financial risks, leaving reputational issues aside.
options.C: since both investments were small in overall scheme and had limited financial and liquidity risk implications for the fund, more consideration could have been given to identifying potential reputational risks and ESG.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The direction is MORE emphasis, not less.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Precisely because both investments were financially small for the fund, reputational/ESG dimensions deserved comparatively more attention.
rationales.C: Correct. Per the reading's guideline answer: 'Finally, since both investments were quite small in the overall scheme and had limited financial and liquidity risk implications for the fund, more consideration could have been given to identifying potential reputational risks and ESG.' The Atsui/ABC case demonstrates how a small direct investment can create outsized reputational and ESG concerns.

options.A: the board setting overall risk tolerance for the organization consistent with its objectives and constraints — risk tolerance captures amount of market risk the institution is willing and able to take to maximize expected returns.
options.B: The general ERM framework begins with management setting risk levels independently of the board.
options.C: The framework begins with external consultants defining the organization's risk appetite.

rationales.A:
Full statement: the board setting overall risk tolerance for the organization consistent with its objectives and constraints — risk tolerance captures amount of market risk the institution is willing and able to take to maximize expected returns; informs the strategic asset allocation.

Correct. Per the reading's ERM section: 'The risk management process for an institutional investor starts with the board setting the overall risk tolerance for the organization that is consistent with its objectives and constraints. Risk tolerance should capture the amount of market risk that an institutional investor is willing and able to take in order to maximize expected returns, and it informs the most important investment decision that is made by the board — namely, the strategic asset allocation.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The BOARD initiates — management operates within the tolerance the board sets.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Consultants may advise, but risk tolerance consistent with objectives and constraints is a board determination.

options.A: Risk tolerance can be expressed in one form only, identical across all institution types.
options.B: asset-only terms (for SWFs, endowments, and foundations) or asset/liability terms (for pension funds and insurance companies).
options.C: Risk tolerance has no formal expression in institutional governance.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Two forms exist, matched to institution type.
rationales.B:
Full statement: asset-only terms (for SWFs, endowments, and foundations) or asset/liability terms (for pension funds and insurance companies) — depending on investor type.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Risk tolerance can be expressed in asset-only (for sovereign wealth funds, endowments, and foundations) or asset/liability terms (for pension funds and insurance companies). Typical risk measures used for setting the risk tolerance of institutional investors include volatility, maximum drawdown, and value at risk or conditional value at risk (sometimes referred to as expected tail loss, or ETL).'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Formal expression is standard — asset-only terms for SWFs/endowments/foundations, asset/liability terms for pensions and insurers.

options.A: Board-approved risk parameters codified in an IPS consist of market predictions for the coming year.
options.B: IPS risk parameters are limited to return targets, with no constraint content.
options.C: liquidity risk parameters, active risk budget to limit and control active management, restrictions on leverage and derivatives, ethical investment guidelines, credit risk parameters and constraints for significant fixed-income investments.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Predictions are not governance parameters.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The IPS carries constraints — liquidity risk parameters, an active risk budget, and restrictions on leverage and derivatives.
rationales.C: Correct. Per the reading: 'In addition to setting the overall risk tolerance (for market losses), the board usually approves additional risk parameters, limits, requirements, and guidelines (some quantitative and others procedural) that are codified in an investment policy statement (IPS). These may include liquidity risk parameters if the institutional investor has a significant allocation to illiquid asset classes, an active risk budget to limit and control the amount of active management pursued by investment staff, restrictions on leverage and the use of derivatives, ethical investment guidelines, and possibly credit risk parameters and constraints in the case of significant fixed-income investments.'

options.A: Asset allocation drift self-corrects over time without intervention.
options.B: gets overlooked — the investment portfolio should be rebalanced on a regular basis to bring it back in line with the strategic asset allocation approved by the board.
options.C: Asset allocation drift always moves the portfolio toward a more optimal position.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Drift compounds without discipline — that is why it gets flagged.
rationales.B: Correct. Per the reading: 'Another risk that gets overlooked is asset allocation drift. The investment portfolio should be rebalanced on a regular basis to bring it back in line with the strategic asset allocation that was approved by the board.' Rebalancing is a systematic risk management action.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Drift is unintended risk migration; regular rebalancing to the strategic allocation is the remedy.

options.A: USD 100 trillion — pension plans account for approximately US$57T (roughly half), SWFs US$8T, endowments/foundations US$1.6T, and banks/insurers net financial assets on order of US$9T.
options.B: Global asset management industry AUM reached approximately USD 10 trillion as of 2020.
options.C: Global asset management industry AUM reached approximately USD 500 trillion as of 2020.

rationales.A: Correct. Per the reading: 'On a global basis, the total value of assets under management (AUM) by the global asset management industry as of 2020 reached more than USD100 trillion, and, as such, wields significant influence over capital markets... Pension plans, which account for approximately US$57 trillion in investable assets or roughly half of global institutional assets under management... Sovereign wealth funds, which account for about US$8 trillion in assets as of the end of 2020... Endowments and foundations, which account for approximately US$1.6 trillion in assets... Banks and insurance companies, comprising net financial assets on the order of US$9 trillion.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The figure is an order of magnitude larger — around USD 100 trillion, with pensions roughly half.
rationales.C: Incorrect. USD 500 trillion overstates the industry several-fold; USD 100 trillion is the 2020 scale.

options.A: The smallest institutional investors face no scale-related constraints in portfolio construction, since fund platforms and intermediaries now give every institution identical access regardless of asset size.
options.B: The smallest institutions enjoy exactly the same access to investment opportunities as the largest funds, because managers waive minimum commitments for any investor with institutional status.
options.C: challenges building diversified portfolios spanning public and private asset classes (unable to access investments with high minimum size like private equity or real estate), difficulty hiring skilled investment professionals, and greater reliance on external asset managers and investment consultants.

rationales.A: Incorrect. High minimums and resource requirements constrain small funds materially.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Access differs sharply — small funds struggle to span public and private classes with diversification.
rationales.C: Correct. Per the reading: 'Institutional investors' assets under management can range from relatively small (e.g., less than US$25 million) to relatively large (e.g., more than US$10 billion). Smaller institutions may face challenges in building a diversified portfolio spanning public and private asset classes because they may be unable to access certain investments that have a high minimum investment size. For example, smaller institutions are less likely to be able to invest in private equity or real estate assets (i.e., property). Small institutional investors may also face challenges in hiring skilled investment professionals. As a result, they are more likely to outsource investments to external asset managers and rely on investment consultants.'

options.A: diseconomies of scale — may be unable to invest in niche investments like venture capital (VC) where small allocations may not generate sufficient returns to justify due diligence costs.
options.B: The largest institutional investors always enjoy favorable economies of scale with no offsetting costs.
options.C: The largest institutions face no scale-related constraints of any kind.

rationales.A:
Full statement: diseconomies of scale — may be unable to invest in niche investments like venture capital (VC) where small allocations may not generate sufficient returns to justify due diligence costs; some strategies are capacity constrained; large trading orders incur market impact costs.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The largest institutional investors, however, may experience dis-economies of scale. For example, they might be unable to invest in certain niche investments like venture capital ("VC"). Given the huge asset size of investments under management, a small allocation to VC may not generate sufficient returns to justify the position (including due diligence costs). The largest institutional investors may also be unable to deploy as much capital as desired with some external managers as certain investment strategies are capacity constrained... To overcome these constraints, some of the largest institutions buy private companies, property, and infrastructure assets directly and manage their traditional asset-class portfolios in-house. Large institutional investors also face the costs of market impact given their sizable trading orders.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. DIS-economies emerge at the top end.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Niche strategies like venture capital become impractical — allocations small enough to fit cannot move the total portfolio.

options.A: Banks and insurance companies are less regulated than other institutional investors.
options.B: Banks and insurers operate with no regulatory oversight of their investment activities.
options.C: tend to be much more asset/liability focused while operating within tight regulations designed to ensure adequacy of capital.

rationales.A: Incorrect. They are MORE tightly regulated, with capital adequacy rules shaping investment behavior.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Oversight is extensive — the asset/liability focus operates inside strict capital regulation.
rationales.C:
Full statement: tend to be much more asset/liability focused while operating within tight regulations designed to ensure adequacy of capital — vs pensions/endowments/SWFs which are more asset-focused with liability considerations less central.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Banks and insurance companies, however, tend to be much more asset/liability focused while operating within tight regulations designed to ensure adequacy of capital.' Banks and insurance companies function as financial intermediaries balancing assets, loans, and derivatives to meet claims of depositors, counterparties, policyholders, and creditors while ensuring adequate capital returns.

options.A: The principal-agent problem arises only when no conflicts of interest exist between parties.
options.B: one person (the agent) makes decisions on behalf of another (the principal) and their interests are not aligned — internal agents include investment committee members and staff.
options.C: The principal-agent problem is avoided because principals make all investment decisions themselves.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The problem IS the conflict — agents deciding for principals with misaligned interests.
rationales.B:
Full statement: one person (the agent) makes decisions on behalf of another (the principal) and their interests are not aligned — internal agents include investment committee members and staff; external agents include third-party asset managers, broker/dealers, consultants, and board members.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The principal–agent issue arises if one person, the agent, makes decisions on behalf of another person or institution, the principal, and their interests are not aligned. A dilemma exists for the agent when he/she may be motivated to act in his/her own best interests and not in the best interests of the principal. Because of operational and investment complexity, institutional investors generally rely on various parties (i.e., agents) to act on their behalf. Agents may be internal or external. Internal agents include investment committee members and investment staff. External agents include third-party asset managers, broker/dealers, consultants, and board members.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Institutional investing relies pervasively on agents (staff, external managers); that reliance creates the problem.

options.A: The hedge fund fee example illustrates perfect alignment between manager and investor interests.
options.B: The fee structure example shows that no conflicts exist in asset-management compensation.
options.C: high base fees due regardless of fund performance give little incentive for the fund manager to produce superior performance.

rationales.A: Incorrect. It illustrates MISALIGNMENT.
rationales.B: Incorrect. High base fees paid regardless of performance blunt the manager's incentive to deliver superior results.
rationales.C:
Full statement: high base fees due regardless of fund performance give little incentive for the fund manager to produce superior performance — such arrangements are common among hedge funds and have led to greater demand for fee transparency and alignment of interest between hedge fund managers and their clients.

Correct. Per the reading: 'A typical example of the principal–agent problem is where performance fee structures are designed by external fund managers to provide attractive compensation to them via a high base fee, which is due regardless of fund performance. This fee structure gives little incentive for the fund manager to produce superior performance. Such fee arrangements are common among hedge funds and have led to greater demand for fee transparency and alignment of interest between hedge fund managers and their clients.'

options.A: mission, investment objectives, and guidelines — plus asset allocation policy with ranges and asset class benchmarks, rebalancing policy, guidelines affecting implementation, and reporting requirements.
options.B: An institutional IPS codifies day-to-day trading strategies and individual security selections.
options.C: An institutional IPS primarily documents management compensation arrangements.

rationales.A:
Full statement: mission, investment objectives, and guidelines — plus asset allocation policy with ranges and asset class benchmarks, rebalancing policy, guidelines affecting implementation, and reporting requirements; helps minimize principal-agent challenges.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Institutional investors codify their mission, investment objectives, and guidelines in an Investment Policy Statement (IPS). The IPS establishes policies and procedures for the effective administration and management of the institutional assets. A well-crafted IPS can help minimize principal–agent challenges by providing clear guidance on day-to-day management of the assets. Besides mission and investment objectives (i.e., return and risk tolerance), the IPS should cover any constraints that affect the asset allocation, asset allocation policy with ranges and asset class benchmarks, rebalancing policy, guidelines affecting the implementation of the asset allocation policy, and reporting requirements.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Trading tactics sit below the IPS; the document sets mission, objectives, allocation policy, ranges, benchmarks, and rebalancing.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Compensation is a governance matter outside the IPS's investment-policy scope.

options.A: For banks and insurers, the investment objective is simply to maximize expected return on the asset portfolio.
options.B: maximize net present value by balancing (i) expected returns on assets, (ii) expected cost of liabilities, (iii) overall risks of assets and liabilities, and (iv) economic relationships between and among assets and liabilities.
options.C: For banks and insurers, the objective is to minimize the volatility of asset values.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Return maximization alone ignores the liability side and capital rules.
rationales.B: Correct. Per the reading: 'For banks and insurance companies, the investment objective is to maximize net present value by balancing (i) the expected returns on assets, (ii) the expected cost of liabilities, (iii) the overall risks of assets and liabilities, and (iv) the economic relationships between and among assets and liabilities.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The objective is NPV maximization balancing asset returns, liability costs, and overall risk within regulatory capital constraints.

options.A: almost exclusive reliance on public equities and fixed income (traditional 60/40), largely passively managed, tight tracking error limits, very little to no alternatives.
options.B: The Norway Model is characterized by heavy exposure to alternatives and private markets.
options.C: The Norway Model holds no public equity exposure.

rationales.A:
Full statement: almost exclusive reliance on public equities and fixed income (traditional 60/40), largely passively managed, tight tracking error limits, very little to no alternatives; advantages: low cost/fees, transparent, low manager selection risk; disadvantage: limited value-added.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Norway model popularized by Norway's global pension fund, Government Pension Fund Global (GPFG). The Norway model is characterized by an almost exclusive reliance on public equities and fixed income (the traditional 60/40 equity/bond model falls under the Norway model), with largely passively managed assets and with very little to no allocation to alternative investments. Investments are usually managed with tight tracking error limits. The advantages of this approach are that investment costs/fees are low, investments are transparent, the risk of poor manager selection is low, and there is little complexity for a governing board. The disadvantage is that there is limited potential for value-added.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Alternatives-heavy investing is the ENDOWMENT model; Norway is nearly all public markets.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Public equities dominate — a largely passive 60/40-style structure with tight tracking error limits.

options.A: The Endowment Model is passive, benchmark-hugging, and holds no alternative investments.
options.B: significant exposure to alternative investments (private markets, hedge funds), significant active management, and externally managed assets.
options.C: The Endowment Model uses no external managers, running everything internally.

rationales.A: Incorrect. That describes the Norway Model — the endowment approach is its opposite.
rationales.B:
Full statement: significant exposure to alternative investments (private markets, hedge funds), significant active management, and externally managed assets; pros: high value-added potential; cons: expensive and difficult to implement for most SWFs due to their large asset sizes.

Correct. Per Exhibit 1: 'Endowment Model: Characterized by high alternatives exposure, active management and outsourcing. Pros: High value-added potential. Cons: Expensive and difficult to implement for most sovereign wealth funds because of their large asset sizes.' The model was popularized by Yale under David Swensen.
rationales.C: Incorrect. External management is characteristic: alternatives-heavy, actively managed, externally implemented.

options.A: P&C insurer liabilities are long-duration and highly predictable in timing and amount.
options.B: P&C insurers carry no meaningful liabilities against their investment portfolios.
options.C: shorter duration and more volatile — involves events with lower probability of occurrence and potentially higher cost (natural disasters like hurricanes).

rationales.A: Incorrect. Long, predictable duration describes LIFE insurers; P&C is short and volatile.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Claims are the liabilities — low-frequency, potentially high-severity events like natural disasters.
rationales.C:
Full statement: shorter duration and more volatile — involves events with lower probability of occurrence and potentially higher cost (natural disasters like hurricanes); volatile claims; short-duration high-uncertainty liability stream; benefit from global diversified portfolios that support statistical analysis via law of large numbers.

Correct. Per the reading: 'In general, P&C insurers face a shorter duration liability stream and investment horizon than life insurers. Further, P&C insurance involves events with lower probability of occurrence and potentially higher cost (especially in the case of natural disasters), leading to highly volatile business claims. This results in a liability stream with short duration and high uncertainty. For example, a P&C insurance company may initiate policies against catastrophic events, such as hurricanes or other natural disasters... P&C insurers may benefit from developing global, diversified portfolios that are more applicable to statistical analysis because of the law of large numbers.'

options.A: often experience negative cash flow (benefit payments exceed contributions) and must hold more cash and other liquid investments compared to open mature plans.
options.B: Frozen DB plans have low liquidity needs because no new benefits accrue.
options.C: Frozen DB plans have no liquidity needs of any kind.

rationales.A:
Full statement: often experience negative cash flow (benefit payments exceed contributions) and must hold more cash and other liquid investments compared to open mature plans; often in de-risking mode.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Frozen DB pension plans, often facing negative cash flow, must hold even more cash and other liquid investments compared to open mature plans.' The more mature a pension fund, the shorter its investment horizon, which directly affects risk tolerance and the allocation between fixed-income assets and riskier assets.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Needs are HIGHER — benefit payments continue while contributions shrink, producing negative cash flow.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Ongoing benefit payments create significant, persistent liquidity demands met with more cash and liquid holdings.

options.A: (1) allows charitable organizations flexibility in spending decisions that could be adjusted for market fluctuations, letting endowments meet fiduciary standard of prudence by maintaining purchasing power.
options.B: UPMIFA eliminated charitable organizations' flexibility in spending decisions.
options.C: UPMIFA made no changes to how endowments are managed or spent.

rationales.A:
Full statement: (1) allows charitable organizations flexibility in spending decisions that could be adjusted for market fluctuations, letting endowments meet fiduciary standard of prudence by maintaining purchasing power; (2) modernizes standard of prudence for management of charitable funds by adopting principles of Modern Portfolio Theory (MPT) established by UPIA (1994).

Correct. Per the reading: 'In the United States, endowments and foundations are governed by the Uniform Prudent Management of Institutional Funds Act of 2006 (UPMIFA). Two important features of UPMIFA include: 1. Allowing charitable organizations flexibility in spending decisions, which could be adjusted for fluctuations in the market value of assets. Endowments, particularly, could meet the fiduciary standard of prudence by maintaining purchasing power of the fund. 2. Modernizing the standard of prudence for the management of charitable funds by adopting the principles of MPT established by the Uniform Prudent Investor Act (1994).'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The act EXPANDED flexibility, allowing spending adjusted for market fluctuations.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Its two features — spending flexibility and prudent-management standards — reshaped US endowment governance.

options.A: IORP II imposes no regulation on European occupational retirement institutions.
options.B: IORP II made no changes to the rules that preceded it.
options.C: governance, risk management, and disclosure — key functions defined (e.g., internal audit), standards for those executing key functions (must not carry similar function for plan sponsor).

rationales.A: Incorrect. The directive is substantive regulation.
rationales.B: Incorrect. IORP II updated the framework around governance, risk management, and disclosure — defining key functions like internal audit and standards for those executing them.
rationales.C:
Full statement: governance, risk management, and disclosure — key functions defined (e.g., internal audit), standards for those executing key functions (must not carry similar function for plan sponsor); requires 'own risk assessment' covering risk of not meeting benefit obligations and operational risk (including administrative error or fraud); greater harmonization of pension benefit statements.

Correct. Per the reading: 'In Europe the updated Institutions for Occupational Retirement Provision (IORP II) will lead to regulatory changes for pension plans. Although each country will interpret the provisions slightly differently, the changes relate to governance, risk management, and disclosure. A number of key functions are defined, such as an internal audit, and standards are applied to those executing these key functions, including a requirement that such a person does not carry out a similar function for the plan sponsor. Many pension plans will need to document their risk management policies and procedures. For example, each fund must document its "own risk assessment" covering items such as the risk of not meeting benefit obligations and operational risk, including administrative error or fraud.'

options.A: leverage limits, enhanced collateral requirements, increased liquidity requirements, central clearing, proprietary trading limits (Volcker Rule), private equity limits, trading tax implementation, brokerage fee limits, compensation limits, and requirements for more transparent reporting.
options.B: Post-GFC regulatory reforms focused primarily on loosening rules to restore market liquidity, rolling back capital requirements and permitting banks greater proprietary trading freedom.
options.C: Post-GFC reforms eliminated regulatory oversight of large financial institutions, transferring supervision entirely to industry self-regulatory organizations.

rationales.A: Correct. Per the reading: 'The key drivers of the legal and regulatory frameworks faced by institutional investors are investor protection, safety and soundness of financial institutions, and integrity of financial markets. Changes to these frameworks following the 2007–2009 global financial crisis focused on leverage limits, enhanced collateral requirements, increased liquidity requirements, central clearing, proprietary trading limits, private equity limits, trading tax implementation, brokerage fee limits, compensation limits, and requirements for more transparent reporting.' Examples: Dodd-Frank/Volcker Rule (US), FATCA (US), UCITS V (EU), AIFMD (EU), Solvency II (EU), MIFID II (EU), EMIR (EU), FTT (EU), PRIIPs (EU).
rationales.B: Incorrect. The direction was TIGHTENING, not loosening.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Oversight expanded — leverage limits, collateral and liquidity requirements, central clearing, and proprietary-trading restrictions.

options.A: A DB plan's funded status has no effect on its risk tolerance.
options.B: Higher funded status implies lower risk tolerance because the surplus must be protected at all costs.
options.C: higher pension surplus or higher funded status implies potentially greater risk tolerance — a well-funded plan has cushion to absorb investment losses.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Funded status is a primary tolerance driver.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The relationship is positive — surplus provides cushion to absorb losses, supporting GREATER risk tolerance.
rationales.C:
Full statement: higher pension surplus or higher funded status implies potentially greater risk tolerance — a well-funded plan has cushion to absorb investment losses; underfunded plans face pressure to grow assets faster than liabilities.

Correct. Per Exhibit 5: 'Plan funded status (surplus or deficit): Higher pension surplus or higher funded status implies potentially greater risk tolerance.' A plan with a surplus (assets > liabilities) can accept more risk since drawdowns won't immediately trigger funding shortfalls; an underfunded plan needs conservative allocation to avoid worsening deficits.

options.A: Asset-liability management studies for DB pension plans are conducted daily as part of routine risk monitoring, with each day's market close triggering a full liability revaluation and simulation cycle.
options.B: Asset-liability management studies are never conducted for DB pension plans, since liability values change too slowly to justify formal periodic analysis.
options.C: every 3-5 years — with Monte Carlo simulations of thousands of scenarios for asset returns and factors driving pension liabilities (importantly the discount rate) aimed at producing probability distributions for funded ratios and contribution rates at different horizons.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The full study is far less frequent than daily monitoring.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The cadence is every 3-5 years, with Monte Carlo simulation of thousands of asset-return and liability-driver scenarios.
rationales.C:
Full statement: every 3-5 years — with Monte Carlo simulations of thousands of scenarios for asset returns and factors driving pension liabilities (importantly the discount rate) aimed at producing probability distributions for funded ratios and contribution rates at different horizons; key metrics include expected funded ratio in 10 or 15 years, surplus volatility, surplus-at-risk, and volatility of contribution rates.

Correct. Per the reading: 'In setting overall investment strategy, many DB pension plans engage in detailed Asset Liability Management studies every 3–5 years. These studies include Monte Carlo simulations of thousands of scenarios for asset returns and factors driving pension liabilities (importantly, the discount rate) aimed at producing probability distributions for funded ratios and contribution rates at different horizons. These distributions are useful for determining key metrics, such as the expected funded ratio in 10 or 15 years, surplus volatility, surplus-at-risk, and volatility of contribution rates.'

options.A: Correlation between sponsor operating results and pension asset returns has no effect on risk tolerance.
options.B: the lower the correlation of sponsor operating results with pension asset returns, the greater the risk tolerance, all else equal.
options.C: Higher correlation between sponsor results and pension assets implies greater risk tolerance.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The common-risk channel is directly relevant.
rationales.B:
Full statement: the lower the correlation of sponsor operating results with pension asset returns, the greater the risk tolerance, all else equal — DB plan asset allocation should be modified to avoid significant exposure to assets highly correlated with sponsor's core business or industry.

Correct. Per Exhibit 5: 'Sponsor and pension fund common risk exposures: Correlation of sponsor operating results with pension asset returns. The lower the correlation, the greater the risk tolerance, all else equal.' Per the reading: 'If the plan sponsor's revenues are highly cyclical, it will not want plan funded status to deteriorate when the core business suffers from a cyclical downturn. In such cases, the DB plan's asset allocation would be modified to ensure adequate diversification so as not to have significant exposure to assets highly correlated with the sponsor's core business or industry.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The direction is opposite — LOWER correlation supports greater tolerance, since sponsor stress and plan losses are less likely to coincide.

options.A: increasing tolerance — younger workforce and higher proportion of active lives means greater duration of plan liabilities and greater risk tolerance.
options.B: A younger workforce and higher proportion of active lives reduce a DB plan's risk tolerance.
options.C: Workforce characteristics have no effect on DB plan risk tolerance.

rationales.A:
Full statement: increasing tolerance — younger workforce and higher proportion of active lives means greater duration of plan liabilities and greater risk tolerance; more mature plans (higher proportion of retirees) have lower risk tolerance and shorter horizons.

Correct. Per Exhibit 5: 'Workforce characteristics: Age of workforce, active lives relative to retired lives. The younger the workforce and the greater the proportion of active lives, the greater the duration of plan liabilities and the greater the risk tolerance.' Also: 'Generally, the more mature a pension fund, the shorter its investment horizon, which directly affects risk tolerance and the allocation between fixed-income assets and riskier assets.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The effect runs positive — youth extends liability duration and the horizon for risk-taking.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Workforce age and the active/retired mix are standard tolerance drivers.

options.A: Sponsor cyclicality has no bearing on how the DB plan's assets should be invested.
options.B: A highly cyclical sponsor should concentrate plan investments in its own industry, where it has expertise.
options.C: if sponsor's revenues are highly cyclical, avoid having plan investments highly correlated with sponsor's core business or industry.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Correlated stress between sponsor and plan is the risk to manage.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Concentration compounds the risk — cyclical sponsors should AVOID plan investments correlated with the core business.
rationales.C:
Full statement: if sponsor's revenues are highly cyclical, avoid having plan investments highly correlated with sponsor's core business or industry — plan sponsor doesn't want funded status to deteriorate when core business suffers from a cyclical downturn.

Correct. Per the reading: 'If the plan sponsor's revenues are highly cyclical, it will not want plan funded status to deteriorate when the core business suffers from a cyclical downturn. In such cases, the DB plan's asset allocation would be modified to ensure adequate diversification so as not to have significant exposure to assets highly correlated with the sponsor's core business or industry. In sum, it is desirable for plan assets to have low (high) correlations with the sponsor's operating assets (liabilities).'

options.A: is the risk that an individual will live longer than expected and draw more in benefit payments than the amount determined in plan liabilities.
options.B: Longevity risk affects only insurance companies, not defined benefit pension plans.
options.C: Longevity risk is not applicable to retirement systems of any type.

rationales.A:
Full statement: is the risk that an individual will live longer than expected and draw more in benefit payments than the amount determined in plan liabilities; pooled in DB plans (early deaths leave assets for those who live longer); pooling doesn't mitigate the effect of rising life expectancies, which implies all else equal increases total DB plan liabilities.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Retired workers also influence the longevity risk of DB plans. Longevity risk is the risk that an individual will live longer than expected and draw more in benefit payments than the amount determined in the calculation of plan liabilities. In private DB pension plans, longevity risk is pooled such that if a participant dies earlier than expected, he/she leaves more assets in the pool that can then cover additional payments for those who live longer than expected. However, this pooling of longevity risk does not mitigate the effect of rising life expectancies, which implies, all else equal, an increase in total DB plan liabilities.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. DB plans bear it directly — retirees living longer draw more benefits than assumed.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The risk is central to any promise of lifetime income.

options.A: mission, investment objectives (return and risk tolerance), constraints affecting asset allocation, asset allocation policy with ranges and asset class benchmarks, rebalancing policy, guidelines affecting implementation, and reporting requirements.
options.B: A well-designed institutional IPS covers the asset allocation policy alone, leaving mission, objectives, and rebalancing rules to separate operational documents maintained by staff.
options.C: A well-designed IPS covers portfolio construction mechanics alone, specifying security selection criteria while leaving governance and objectives undocumented.

rationales.A: Correct. Per the reading: 'Besides mission and investment objectives (i.e., return and risk tolerance), the IPS should cover any constraints that affect the asset allocation, asset allocation policy with ranges and asset class benchmarks, rebalancing policy, guidelines affecting the implementation of the asset allocation policy, and reporting requirements. The IPS should be reviewed annually; however, revisions should be infrequent, such as when material changes occur in investor circumstances and/or the market environment, as the IPS serves as the foundation for the investment program.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Allocation is one chapter — mission, objectives, risk tolerance, constraints, benchmarks, and rebalancing complete it.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Construction detail sits below the IPS; the document is the comprehensive governance charter.

options.A: Institutional IPS documents are never reviewed once adopted, since the original drafting process is expected to anticipate all future circumstances.
options.B: review annually; revisions should be infrequent, such as when material changes occur in investor circumstances and/or market environment — since IPS serves as foundation for investment program.
options.C: The IPS should be revised weekly to reflect market movements, ensuring the policy portfolio continuously tracks current valuations and sentiment.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Annual review is the standard practice.
rationales.B:
Full statement: review annually; revisions should be infrequent, such as when material changes occur in investor circumstances and/or market environment — since IPS serves as foundation for investment program; asset allocation policy and investment guidelines typically included in an appendix that can be modified more easily.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The IPS should be reviewed annually; however, revisions should be infrequent, such as when material changes occur in investor circumstances and/or the market environment, as the IPS serves as the foundation for the investment program. The asset allocation policy and investment guidelines are typically included in an appendix that can be modified more easily.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. REVIEW is annual; REVISION is infrequent, reserved for material changes in circumstances or environment.

options.A: The corporate DB plan IPS objective is to always maximize returns without qualification.
options.B: The corporate DB plan IPS objective is to minimize plan costs regardless of return outcomes.
options.C: 'wishing to maximize the long-term return on investments subject to, in its opinion, an acceptably low likelihood of failing to achieve an ongoing 105% funding level'.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Maximization is CONDITIONED on an acceptably low likelihood of failing to meet obligations.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Cost minimization alone is not the objective; long-term return subject to funding security is.
rationales.C:
Full statement: 'wishing to maximize the long-term return on investments subject to, in its opinion, an acceptably low likelihood of failing to achieve an ongoing 105% funding level' — balancing sustainable return with acceptable funded ratio risk.

Correct. Per the reading's example Corporate DB Pension Plan IPS: 'The Trustee wishes to ensure that the Corporate Plan can meet its obligations to the beneficiaries while recognizing the cost implications to the Company of pursuing excessively conservative investment strategies. The objectives of the Plan are defined as: wishing to maximize the long-term return on investments subject to, in its opinion, an acceptably low likelihood of failing to achieve an ongoing 105% funding level.'

options.A: 'to build an investment portfolio to outperform inflation + 4% per annum over 7-year periods while accepting a high level of risk that is expected to generate 4-6 negative annual returns over any 20-year period'.
options.B: The corporate DC plan growth option specifies no return objective at all.
options.C: The DC growth option promises always-positive returns in every period.

rationales.A:
Full statement: 'to build an investment portfolio to outperform inflation + 4% per annum over 7-year periods while accepting a high level of risk that is expected to generate 4-6 negative annual returns over any 20-year period' — captures return, horizon, and risk transparency.

Correct. Per the reading's Corporate DC Pension Plan IPS example: 'For example, a participants selecting the growth option indicates a higher risk tolerance over a longer investment time horizon. The investment objective for the growth option is to build an investment portfolio to outperform inflation + 4% per annum over 7-year periods while accepting a high level of risk that is expected to generate 4–6 negative annual returns over any 20-year period.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. A real objective is stated — inflation + 4% per annum over 7-year periods.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The objective explicitly ACCEPTS a high level of risk, acknowledging negative periods.

options.A: The endowment example IPS contains no spending policy.
options.B: 80% of prior year's spending adjusted for inflation (CPI in range 0-6%) plus 20% of long-term spending rate (5.0%) applied to 12-quarter rolling average of market values.
options.C: The endowment spending policy distributes exactly 5% of current assets every year.

rationales.A: Incorrect. A well-defined hybrid policy is specified.
rationales.B:
Full statement: 80% of prior year's spending adjusted for inflation (CPI in range 0-6%) plus 20% of long-term spending rate (5.0%) applied to 12-quarter rolling average of market values — hybrid rule providing budget stability while adjusting to fluctuating market value.

Correct. Per the reading's endowment IPS example: 'Under this policy, spending for a given year equals 80% of spending in the previous year, adjusted for inflation (CPI within a range of 0% and 6%), plus 20% of the long-term spending rate (5.0%) applied to the 12-quarter rolling average of market values. This spending policy has two implications. First, by incorporating the previous year's spending, the policy eliminates large fluctuations and so enables the University to plan for operating budget needs. Second, by adjusting spending toward a long-term rate of 5.0%, the policy ensures that spending levels will be sensitive to fluctuating market value levels, thereby providing stability in long-term purchasing power.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The rule blends 80% of prior spending (inflation-adjusted, CPI 0-6%) with 20% of the 5.0% long-term rate applied to a 12-quarter average — smoothing, not a flat 5%.

options.A: Institutional IPS documents impose no reporting requirements on investment staff.
options.B: performance measurement and attribution for quarter and trailing periods in absolute terms and relative to established benchmarks, asset allocation of the total portfolio, and market value of the total portfolio.
options.C: IPS reporting is limited to a single annual statement.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Reporting is a standard IPS component.
rationales.B:
Full statement: performance measurement and attribution for quarter and trailing periods in absolute terms and relative to established benchmarks, asset allocation of the total portfolio, and market value of the total portfolio — provided to Board of Trustees, IC, and other stakeholders.

Correct. Per the reading's example IPS: 'The Investment Team, with the oversight of management, must provide adequate reporting to the Board of Trustees, the IC, and other stakeholders. The reporting structure should include the following: Performance measurement and attribution for the quarter and trailing periods for the portfolio both in absolute terms and relative to the established benchmarks; Asset allocation of the total portfolio; Market value of the total portfolio.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The cadence is quarterly and trailing-period — performance measurement and attribution, absolute and benchmark-relative.

options.A: A DB pension plan's primary investment objective involves no target return.
options.B: A DB plan's primary objective is always to minimize risk regardless of funding requirements.
options.C: achieve a long-term target return (usually defined in nominal terms) over specified horizon (3-5 years or as long as 10 or 25 years) with an appropriate level of risk that allows the plan to meet its contractual liabilities.

rationales.A: Incorrect. A long-term target return — usually nominal — anchors the objective.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Risk minimization alone cannot meet the liabilities; the objective is the target return over a specified horizon (3-5, up to 10-25 years).
rationales.C:
Full statement: achieve a long-term target return (usually defined in nominal terms) over specified horizon (3-5 years or as long as 10 or 25 years) with an appropriate level of risk that allows the plan to meet its contractual liabilities; secondary objective could be minimize present value of expected cash contributions.

Correct. Per the reading: 'In summary, the primary objective for DB pension plans is to achieve a long-term target return (usually defined in nominal terms) over a specified investment horizon (3–5 years or even as long as 10 or 25 years) with an appropriate level of risk that allows the plan to meet its contractual liabilities. The secondary objective could be to minimize the present value of expected cash contributions.'

options.A: invest a significant portion in cash and high-grade fixed-income instruments that are very liquid with little risk of significant drawdown.
options.B: Budget stabilization funds invest predominantly in public equities to maximize long-run growth.
options.C: Budget stabilization funds hold no cash, keeping all assets fully invested in risk assets.

rationales.A:
Full statement: invest a significant portion in cash and high-grade fixed-income instruments that are very liquid with little risk of significant drawdown — because they may need to support the government budget on short notice during commodity price shocks or other external shocks.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Stabilization funds must maintain a high level of liquidity and invest in assets that have a low risk of significant losses over short time periods. For example, in the event of a negative commodity price shock, the government might experience a significant budget deficit caused by lower commodity-based revenues. To stabilize the budget and meet spending needs, the stabilization fund's assets must be readily accessible. As a result, budget stabilization funds invest a significant portion of their portfolios in cash and high-grade, fixed-income instruments that are very liquid and carry little risk of significant drawdown.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Their purpose — bridging revenue shortfalls on short notice — demands conservative, liquid assets.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Cash and high-grade liquid fixed income dominate precisely because drawdowns must be avoidable at need.

options.A: Foundations and endowments follow identical approaches with no meaningful differences.
options.B: The reading identifies no differences between foundation and endowment investment management.
options.C: similar investment philosophy despite important differences: foundations legally required to pay out 5% of assets annually (US private grant-making) plus investment expenses vs endowments with more-flexible spending rules.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Legal payout rules alone separate them.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The philosophies are similar, but US private foundations face a legally required ~5% annual payout that endowments do not.
rationales.C:
Full statement: similar investment philosophy despite important differences: foundations legally required to pay out 5% of assets annually (US private grant-making) plus investment expenses vs endowments with more-flexible spending rules; foundations generally have higher liquidity needs; foundations may pursue mission-related/impact investing (Ford Foundation US$1B over 10 years).

Correct. Per the reading: 'In practice, the investment philosophy of private foundations is typically similar to that of university endowments, despite important differences between them in terms of liabilities and liquidity needs. Foundations and endowments both typically have perpetual investment horizons... and both invest to maintain purchasing power; however, foundations generally have higher liquidity needs. In the United States, private grant-making foundations are legally required to pay out 5% of assets (on a trailing 12-month basis) plus investment expenses, while university endowments have more-flexible spending rules.' Also mission-related investing (e.g., 'the Ford Foundation has allocated up to US$1.0 billion (more than 8% of assets) over 10 years to investments related to its mission of addressing global inequality').

options.A: skill sets — university endowments typically have alumni sitting on boards (people with special relationship + potentially significant financial market skills like PE or HF).
options.B: Foundation boards and endowment boards have identical composition and skill sets.
options.C: The reading draws no distinction between foundation and endowment governance.

rationales.A:
Full statement: skill sets — university endowments typically have alumni sitting on boards (people with special relationship + potentially significant financial market skills like PE or HF); foundation board members are typically individuals involved with grant making and not necessarily investment professionals; this difference may affect quality of board oversight, delegation, and investment decision quality.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The boards of foundations tend to be different in terms of skill sets than the boards of endowments. University endowments typically have alumni sitting on their boards — people with a special relationship to the university and who may have significant financial market skills (for example, in private equity or hedge funds). Board members for foundations, however, are typically individuals involved with grant making and not necessarily investment professionals. This difference in skill sets may affect the quality of board oversight, the level of delegation of decision making to investment staff, and the quality of investment decisions.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Composition differs systematically.
rationales.C: Incorrect. University endowment boards feature alumni with special relationships and often significant investment expertise — a distinct profile.

options.A: large differences by country — Australia and US have largest proportions of DC pension assets and largest allocations to equities.
options.B: The P7 country data show identical pension asset allocations across all seven countries.
options.C: The P7 data show no equity allocation in any country's pension system.

rationales.A:
Full statement: large differences by country — Australia and US have largest proportions of DC pension assets and largest allocations to equities; US, Australia, and Netherlands have highest proportions of equity allocations in domestic markets (significant home bias); Japan has been slowest to increase alternatives.

Correct. Per the reading: 'An examination of pension fund asset allocations shows very large differences in average asset allocations by country... Australia and the US have the largest proportions of DC pension assets and also the largest allocations to equities. Although not shown in Exhibit 6, it is worth noting that the United States, Australia, and the Netherlands have the highest proportions of their equities allocations invested in their local markets. Given the size of the domestic equities markets in Australia and the Netherlands, this implies significant home bias... Japan has been slowest among the select countries to increase allocations to alternatives; however, the transition is underway with the country's largest pension plan, Government Pension Investment Fund (GPIF), which is reducing its allocation to domestic bonds in favor of alternatives.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Variation is the exhibit's finding.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Equities feature heavily — Australia and the US show the largest DC proportions and equity allocations.

options.A: Endowments experienced no liquidity impact during the 2007-2009 financial crisis.
options.B: During the GFC, endowments found themselves holding excess liquidity they could not deploy.
options.C: significant liquidity challenges — several large endowments were forced to (1) sell portions of private investment portfolios in secondary markets, (2) reduce payouts to universities, or (3) issue bonds to bridge liquidity needs.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Liquidity stress was severe.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The problem was SHORTFALL — several large endowments were forced into secondary sales of private portfolios at marked-down prices and into emergency borrowing.
rationales.C:
Full statement: significant liquidity challenges — several large endowments were forced to (1) sell portions of private investment portfolios in secondary markets, (2) reduce payouts to universities, or (3) issue bonds to bridge liquidity needs; David Swensen has warned against blanket application of the Endowment Model.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Most large endowments follow the endowment investment model and rely heavily on alternative investments to achieve their long-term investment objectives. This approach is not without risks. During the global financial crisis, several large endowments faced significant liquidity challenges and were forced to either sell portions of their private investment portfolios in the secondary markets, reduce payouts to their universities, or issue bonds to bridge their liquidity needs. The rapid post-crisis recovery arguably bailed out many endowments, but had the crisis lasted longer, the pain would have been substantially worse. David Swensen, the longtime CIO of the Yale Endowment, and his colleagues have regularly warned against a blanket application of the endowment model.'

options.A: Bank asset structures contain no loans, consisting mainly of securities portfolios.
options.B: loans as the largest component (typically comprising up to 50% or more of assets of large international banks that dominate the sector).
options.C: Bank assets are held entirely in cash and central bank reserves.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Loans DOMINATE — typically up to 50% or more of large international banks' assets.
rationales.B:
Full statement: loans as the largest component (typically comprising up to 50% or more of assets of large international banks that dominate the sector); next largest is debt securities (typically 25% or more of total assets); remainder consists of currency, deposits with central banks, receivables, and bullion.

Correct. Per the reading: 'The largest component of bank assets is loans, typically comprising up to 50% or more of the assets of the large, international banks that dominate the sector. The next largest component of assets is debt securities, typically accounting for 25% or more of total assets. The largest remaining portion of assets consists of currency, deposits with central banks (e.g., Bank of Japan or Bank of England), receivables, and bullion.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Cash is a fraction; the loan book is the core earning asset.

options.A: Bank liability structures contain no customer deposits, relying entirely on wholesale funding markets and interbank borrowing for their financing.
options.B: Bank liabilities consist entirely of shareholder equity, with banks prohibited from deposit-taking under post-crisis capital rules.
options.C: deposits are largest component (usually more than half of total liabilities), consisting of time deposits (savings, CDs) and demand deposits (checking, savings accessible via online/ATM).

rationales.A: Incorrect. Deposits are the LARGEST component — usually more than half of total liabilities.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Equity is a thin slice; time deposits (savings, CDs) and demand deposits fund the balance sheet.
rationales.C:
Full statement: deposits are largest component (usually more than half of total liabilities), consisting of time deposits (savings, CDs) and demand deposits (checking, savings accessible via online/ATM); wholesale funding (Fed Funds, public funds); long-term debt 10-15%; trading/repurchase payables 10-20%.

Correct. Per the reading: 'Banks' liabilities are comprised of deposits and also include short-term funding, such as commercial paper, as well as longer term debt. Deposits are the largest component of liabilities, usually more than half of total liabilities. Bank deposits include the following: Time deposits or term deposits − These interest-bearing accounts have a specified maturity date. This category includes savings accounts and certificates of deposit (CDs)... Demand deposits − These accounts can be drawn upon regularly and without notice... In addition to deposits, banks can access wholesale funding, sources of which include Federal Funds, public funds, and other government-supported, short-term vehicles... Other liabilities comprise (1) long-term debt, 10%–15% of total balance sheet; and (2) such items as trading/securities payables and repurchase finance payables, also on the order of 10%–20% of balance sheet liabilities.'

options.A: tactical horizon directly impacted by nature and maturities of asset base and liability structure — instruments held tend to have far shorter maturities than those held by other financial institutions.
options.B: A bank's tactical and strategic investment horizons are identical.
options.C: Banks operate with no tactical horizon in managing their securities portfolios.

rationales.A:
